feed.title: SAP Community - SAP HANA
feed.subtitle: SAP HANA blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Database Operations Through AMDP
entry.content:
Introduction: AMDP (ABAP Managed Database Procedures) is a framework in SAP that allows ABAP developers to write and execute database-specific SQL Script code (mainly for SAP HANA) directly from ABAP classes. It gives ABAP the ability to run complex, high-performance database operations on HANA using SQL Script, instead of pulling data into ABAP memory.

INSERT (Adding Data):
Use the INSERT statement to add new rows to a table. You can insert single rows or multiple
rows using INSERT INTO ... SELECT

METHOD insert_data
  BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
  LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
  USING ZPS_TEST.

    IF NOT EXISTS ( SELECT *
                      FROM ZPS_TEST
                     WHERE eno = :e_no )
    THEN
      INSERT INTO ZPS_TEST VALUES (
          session_context('CLIENT'),
          :e_no,
          :e_name,
          session_context('SAP_SYSTEM_DATE'),
          session_context('APPLICATIONUSER'),
          TO_VARCHAR(CURRENT_TIME, 'HH24MISS'),
          session_context('APPLICATIONUSER'),
          TO_VARCHAR(CURRENT_TIME, 'HH24MISS')
      );

      i_msg = 'Success';
    ELSE
      i_msg = 'Unsuccessful';
    END IF;

  ENDMETHOD.

UPDATE (Modifying Data):
Use the UPDATE statement to modify existing rows in a table. Use the WHERE clause to
specify which rows to update. Be careful not to omit the WHERE clause, as that would
update all rows in the table.

METHOD update_data
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    USING ZPS_TEST.

    IF EXISTS ( SELECT * FROM ZPS_TEST WHERE eno = :e_no ) THEN

      UPDATE ZPS_TEST SET
          ename   = :e_name,
          euperson = session_context('APPLICATIONUSER'),
          eutime   = TO_VARCHAR(CURRENT_TIME, 'HH24MISS')
        WHERE eno = :e_no;

      i_msg = 'Success';
    ELSE
      i_msg = 'Unsuccessful';
    END IF;

  ENDMETHOD.

DELETE (Removing Data):
Use the DELETE statement to remove rows from a table. As with UPDATE, use the WHERE
clause carefully to avoid deleting unintended rows.

METHOD delete_data
    BY DATABASE PROCEDURE FOR HDB
    LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
    USING ZPS_TEST.

    IF EXISTS ( SELECT * FROM ZPS_TEST WHERE eno = :e_no ) THEN

      DELETE FROM ZPS_TEST
        WHERE eno = :e_no;

      i_msg = 'Success';
    ELSE
      i_msg = 'Unsuccessful';
    END IF;

  ENDMETHOD.

Program to Call Above Methods in ABAP:

REPORT zrj_amdp_emp_crud.
PARAMETERS : i_ono TYPE zde_ono OBLIGATORY.
PARAMETERS : i_name TYPE zde_ename MODIF ID a1.

PARAMETERS pr1 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1 DEFAULT 'X'.
PARAMETERS pr2 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1.
PARAMETERS pr3 TYPE c RADIOBUTTON GROUP r1.

DATA : lv_insert  TYPE string.
DATA : lv_update  TYPE string.
DATA : lv_delete  TYPE string.

START-OF-SELECTION.
  IF pr1 = 'X'.
    zps_c_emp=>insert_data(
        EXPORTING
            e_no = i_ono
            e_name = i_name
        IMPORTING
            i_msg = lv_insert
            ).

    IF lv_insert = 'Success' .
      WRITE : TEXT-001 , i_ono.
    ELSE.
      WRITE : TEXT-002 .
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

  IF pr2 = 'X'.
    zps_c_emp=>delete_data(
        EXPORTING
            e_no = i_ono
        IMPORTING
            i_msg = lv_update
            ).

    IF lv_update = 'Success' .
      WRITE : TEXT-003 , i_ono.
    ELSE.
      WRITE : TEXT-004 .
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

  IF pr3 = 'X'.
    zps_c_emp=>update_data(
        EXPORTING
            e_no = i_ono
            e_name = i_name
        IMPORTING
            i_msg = lv_delete
            ).

    IF lv_delete = 'Success' .
      WRITE : TEXT-005 , i_ono.
    ELSE.
      WRITE : TEXT-006 .
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT.
  IF pr3 ='X'.
    LOOP AT SCREEN.
      IF screen-group1 = 'A1'.
        screen-active = 0.
        MODIFY SCREEN.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDIF.

Conclusion:
AMDP allows ABAP developers to perform powerful database operations directly in SAP HANA using SQL Script. By embedding SQL Script inside ABAP classes, AMDP enables fast execution of SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations at the database level, avoiding data transfer to the ABAP layer.

entry.title: How is Column Store fetch faster than Row Store?
entry.content:
Introduction:
This  topic describes the distinction between column-based and row-based data storage and its impact on the query performance. The blog uses a big ORDERS table to compare the cost of accessing data. It also emphasises the fact that row stores can read whole rows when only a few columns are required. This results in unneeded data reads, increased I/O, and increased CPU consumption. It also describes how indexing minimizes the amount of data that is scanned and introduces overhead of random access. Column stores, on the other hand, only read the appropriate columns such as Amount and Country.
This helps a lot in minimizing the overall data read and enhances efficiency.Column store performance further is enhanced by sequential access and compression. The blog is designed to simplify these concepts using real numbers.

Target Audience:
It can be used by developers, data engineers, consultants as well as novices who are learning database systems.It is desirable to have basic knowledge of databases, SQL, and, optionally, SAP HANA, and access to a database system such as SAP S/4HANA or any column-store platform.

 

Body:

Consider a table called ORDERS that has 100,000,000 rows having columns: Amount (8 bytes decimal), OrderDate (8 bytes), Country (dictionary-encoded; code 1 byte), OrderID (4 bytes int), CustomerID (4 bytes int), and five additional miscellaneous columns with an average of 8 bytes each. In a row store, the approximate row width is ≈ 4+4+1+8+8 + (5×8) = 69 bytes, which rounds up to 72 bytes.

Ex: SELECT SUM(Amount) FROM ORDERS WHERE Country = 'IN'.

 

If the executable query is 'IN' = 5% of rows (selectivity 5%), the cost of fetching data from a row store table is as follows: 

Case A: Complete table scan: Bytes read ≈ rows × row_size = 100,000,000 × 72B = 7.2 GB.However, only 11% of that is required by the query, which only requires one column (Amount). Thus, nearly 90% of what is read is meaningless. It also means more CPU work and slower performance.

Case B: Applying a B-tree index to the Country. Using the index, find the 5% matching = 5,000,000 row IDs. The base table's bytes touched are approximately 360 MB, or 5,000,000 × 72B. In addition to the CPU overhead for random pointers, index pages are read in tens to hundreds of MB. Even though there are fewer total bytes than a full scan, performance can still lag behind a tight sequential column scan due to random access + cache misses.

Data fetch cost in a column store table: 

Each column is stored independently in a column-based database. Therefore, it only reads the necessary columns (Country and Amount) for the same query. Bytes read ≈ rows × row_size = Amount column → 100 million × 8 bytes = 800 MB + Country column → 100 million × 1 byte = 100 MB.

To figure out which rows belong to "IN," it first scans the country (100 MB). After that, it adds up only the rows that are marked after scanning the Amount column up to 800 MB. The CPU operates very efficiently because this data is sequentially stored and processed in batches. Parts of the data may even be ignored if it is compressed or grouped, which would lower the read size to less than 900 MB. Therefore, a column store requires only about 900 MB of tightly packed, easily readable data rather than 7.2 GB as a row store does. This makes it approximately eight times faster and much more CPU-friendly.

Conclusion:
In Summary, column stores drastically decrease data reads as only necessary columns are accessed, enhancing performance. They reduce I/O, increase the efficiency of the CPU, and scale to large analytical queries. Row stores are helpful in transactions, but not as effective at such workloads. The correct storage format depends on the use-case to ensure optimum performance. A few best practices include using column stores to perform analytics and query only needed data.

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Understanding SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA): Execution Boundaries and Remote Data Processing
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA) enables SAP HANA to access data stored in external systems without replicating the data into SAP HANA.

External systems are connected as remote sources, and their objects can be represented inside SAP HANA as virtual tables.
From an application perspective, these objects can be queried using standard SQL statements, while the data remains in the source system and is accessed during statement execution.

Official SAP HANA Smart Data Access documentation:
Data Access in the SAP HANA Cloud, SAP HANA Database

This article explains general processing behavior and supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.
It does not provide configuration or performance tuning guidance.


Remote Sources and Virtual Tables
With SDA, SAP HANA integrates remote data access into SQL processing.
When a query references a virtual table, SAP HANA processes the statement, and data access is performed via the connected remote source during execution.
This behavior is part of the documented SAP HANA Smart Data Access architecture.


Query Processing Context
In SAP HANA Smart Data Access, SQL statements referencing virtual tables may involve both local SAP HANA processing and operations executed against the connected remote source.
As part of query processing, operations may be executed locally in SAP HANA or against the connected remote source.
Query execution may therefore include processing steps across system boundaries as part of normal system behavior.


Support-Relevant Distinction
From a support perspective, the following distinction is essential:
A statement can be initiated and processed by SAP HANA, while parts of the effective data processing may take place outside SAP HANA via the remote source and the communication layer.
Because of this execution boundary, the status of SAP HANA services alone does not describe the complete processing path when Smart Data Access is used.


Observations in Troubleshooting
During troubleshooting, it is possible that local SAP HANA processing appears normal while statements accessing virtual tables show different response times.

This can occur because the execution path may include:

  • the SAP HANA database
  • the network communication layer
  • the connected remote source

The overall response time therefore reflects the complete end-to-end processing path.


Documented SDA Scenarios
SAP documentation and SAP Notes describe different SDA-related scenarios that can influence behavior during execution.

SDA FAQ:
https://me.sap.com/notes/2180119

Examples include:

  • Remote source object discovery error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2572731
  • SDA remote source connection test / communication error scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/3470273
  • Remote source object access / closed connection scenario
    https://me.sap.com/notes/2557181

These scenarios may occur while the SAP HANA database itself remains available.


Interpreting Monitoring
When remote sources are involved, troubleshooting should consider the full end-to-end data access path rather than only local database activity.


Support Scope Clarification
When analyzing statements accessing virtual tables, the involved components may include:

  • SAP HANA database
  • network communication
  • remote data source

Observed behavior alone is not sufficient to determine a root cause and requires end-to-end analysis across all involved components.


Conclusion
SAP HANA Smart Data Access allows access to external data without replication.
Virtual tables represent remote objects, and data access from the connected system is part of normal execution behavior.
Statements executed in SAP HANA may therefore include processing steps outside the SAP HANA database, while SAP HANA operates according to its documented architecture.
Understanding this behavior supports correct interpretation during troubleshooting.


Disclaimer
This article summarizes documented system behavior based on publicly available SAP documentation and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

Only official SAP documentation and SAP Notes are authoritative.

entry.title: Configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker for HANA replication
entry.content:
In manual HANA replication configuration.We have to perform Take over/switch over manually.To automate take over/switch over.we have to configure cluster environment using SUSE Pacemaker.

 

Below are the steps
1.NFSSRV2-NFS server
2.HANADBPRI-Primary HANA DB
3.HANADBHA-Secondary/HA HANA DB
4.HANADBVHOST-Virtual IP/Floating IP(It will be moving here based on the active nodes.
Note:Virtual IP once added will not be reachable until Pacemaker is confifgured

Pre-Requisites
1.Install HANA DB
2.Configure replication
3.Below OS packages needs to be installed
zypper install -t pattern ha_sles-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qdevice- Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
zypper in SAPHanaSR-Mandatory
zypper in corosync-qnetd-Not Mandatory.Required only id Q devices are installed
4.Install Primary and Secondary/HA DB with Local host name.

Note:In cluster environment we install HANA DB with local hostname and not with Virtual host name.Whereas Cluster environment with other products like ASCS,ERS we will be install SAP using Virtual Host Name

 

We are using setup as below

 

Scenario 1:

Suse pacemaker will be sending heartbeat from Primary to Secondary/HA DB(Basically it is called as Ping).If we are not getting the reply for the ping.Then Pacemaker will bring up Secondary/HA DB

Scenario 2:

Split Brain scenario-Both Primary and Secondary/HA DB’s will be alive but due to some network issue replication will be broken and no heart beat signal will be passed to each other.Primary DB and Secondary/HA DB thinks that each other DB is down & dead(But both DB will be up) and try to pull up the cluster and bring the resources(Virtual IP/Floating IP) in their respective DB thinking other DB is down.This causes confusion to Suse pacemaker and network.This scenario is called as Split Brain Scenario.


To Avoid the Split Brain Scenario we have 2 solutions


1.SDB-1 shared disk is created in the virtualized platforms and it is mounted in both the platforms and status of the cluster is stored in the SDB and it very small in size almost 1mb.If SDB is disconnected then it the DB will think that network is down.If SDB is available and Hana DB is not available.Then it will think that Hana DB is down and take over/switch over will happen.


2.Q device using NFS Filesystem
Note:When the DB is down the only virtual/Floating IP will be shifted from primary to secondary/HA DB because replication is active and Data will be present in secondary.Where in SAP ASCS/ERS will be installed and complete Filesystem will be moved from Primary to HA server.
Configure replication between Primary & Secondary/HA DB.Take backup for primary and Secondary/HA DB and enable replication.

 

 

Note:
1.Scale Up-Only 1 systemwe can increase the Ram and other resources.
2.Scale Out-We can add the multiple nodes(Mater node->Worker Node->Standy node)

 

Configuration of Cluster

1.This command will work only when the above mentioned packages are installed

 

2.Ring is interface for network communication. It will be created in both HA and DR.It will automatically take up the local IP

 

3.We are following the Qdevice using NFS Server method

 

 

We are going with the Q device method
Note:The below highlighted packages mandatorily needs to be installed in the NFS Server to Run Q devices.

 

 

Current status if cluster-crm status

 

To check the live changes in cluster-crm_mon

 

Check whether watchdog is active or not.It is used to check the services running on OS level

 

Now cluster is initiated in Primary Node.Cluster is the one time activity which needs to be configured only once on 1 node and in another node we can join the existing cluster
Note:Cluster join means any cluster configuration changes made in one server will reflect into another server

 

Check whether watchdog is running or not

 

Watchdog is not running,or else it will show the line as dog
Now start the watchdog
If watchdog is not active create it
echo softdog > /etc/modules-load.d/watchdog.conf
start watchdog
systemctl restart systemd-modules-load.service

 

Put cluster into maintainence mode

 

We need to add hana related resources into the server.It can be added from any node.We need to add the resource agent(RA).RA is the utility used to perform or monitor the operations.


There are 2 resource agent

1.SAP HANA topology(Used to check whether hana services is crashed or not)

2.SAP HANA(Used to start and stop the database) are the two RA.
Note:We can add primitives in either primary or secondary server node.It will automatically sync to another server.But in this scenario I am adding primitive in 1st server.

 

Add the resource agent

 

Add the 1st primitive
primitive rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHanaTopology \ #Monitor the HANA services#
op monitor interval="10" timeout="600" \ #Will monitor the service for every 10 secs and should get an reply within 600 sec#
op start interval="0" timeout="600" \#Hana DB stop and start time#
op stop interval="0" timeout="300" \\#Hana DB stop and start time#
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00"#HANA DB SID and instance number#
clone cln_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 \ #Clone of the HANA topology#

 

Once saved clone is set into secondary

 

SAP HANA Topology resource agent comes with the package zypper SAP HANA SR

 

Add 2nd primitive HANA DB Resource Agent for HANA db start and stop in primary server

 

primitive rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 ocf:suse:SAPHana \
op start interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op stop interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op promote interval="0" timeout="3600" \
op monitor interval="60" role="Master" timeout="700" \
op monitor interval="61" role="Slave" timeout="700" \
params SID="HDB" InstanceNumber="00" PREFER_SITE_TAKEOVER="true" \
DUPLICATE_PRIMARY_TIMEOUT="7200" AUTOMATED_REGISTER="true" \
meta priority="100"
ms msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 rsc_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00 \
meta clone-max="2" clone-node-max="1" interleave="true"

 

 

save

 

Add constraints into the primary server

Two constraints are organizing the correct placement of the virtual IP address for the client database access and the start order between the two resource agents SAPHana and SAPHanaTopology.
colocation col_admin-ip_HDB_HDB00 2000: admin-ip:Started \
msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00:Master
order ord_SAPHana_HA1_HDB10 Optional: cln_SAPHanaTop_HDB_HDB00 \
msl_SAPHana_HDB_HDB00

 

This will not do any changes after saving as it is only rules.
Note:The extra lines will appear after adding primitive 1 and primitive 2 and saving it in the file

 

 

Note:

 

Cluster delete command

Note if you are stuck inbetween and want to start from first you need to remove the cluster by below

Next part will be testing which will be continued in next blog

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA
entry.content:
A lot of SAP professionals learn database administration while juggling project deadlines, system issues, and last-minute troubleshooting.  Honestly, that’s how many of the best lessons happen.

But having structured guidance changes the pace completely.

Our new Partner Learning Accelerator for the SAP Certified - Database Administrator - SAP HANA certification was created to help partners prepare with more clarity, more practice, and fewer surprises on exam day.

Across two live sessions, we’ll walk through topics that database administrators deal with every day:

  • Provisioning SAP HANA Cloud databases
  • Monitoring and configuration
  • Backup and recovery
  • Startup and shutdown activities
  • Migration paths to SAP HANA Cloud

Plus, we’ll connect the technical concepts with exam-relevant scenarios and project participation tips.

If you’re planning your next certification milestone, or helping your team deepen SAP HANA expertise, this session series is worth adding to your calendar.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013387.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPCertification #SAPHANACloud #TechSkills #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: Preparing Operational Context Before Opening an SAP HANA Support Incident
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA environments may involve multiple technical layers, including database services, application components, operating systems, storage, networking, monitoring, and high-availability mechanisms.

When operational issues occur, the initially visible symptom does not always identify the originating technical layer responsible for the observed behavior. Effective investigation therefore often depends not only on the observed symptom itself, but also on the quality and structure of the operational context collected during the initial investigation phase.

SAP provides self-service resources through SAP Help Portal documentation, SAP Notes, SAP Knowledge Base Articles, SAP HANA cockpit, SAP for Me, Guided Answers, troubleshooting guides, and related support resources.

At the same time, many operational situations still require system-specific investigation by SAP Support.

 

This article summarizes a documentation-based preparation approach for SAP HANA incident handling using publicly available SAP troubleshooting concepts and support resources.

It does not provide troubleshooting instructions, root-cause conclusions, configuration recommendations, or performance tuning guidance.

Its purpose is to support clearer communication, reduce unnecessary clarification cycles, and help investigation activities focus more efficiently on the affected technical area.

Observable Symptoms and Technical Interpretation
During incident situations, operational discussions sometimes begin with broad conclusions such as:

  • “The database is down.”
  • “System Replication failed.”
  • “Performance is degraded.”
  • “Recovery is broken.”
  • “Monitoring indicates the database is healthy.”

However, SAP HANA landscapes may involve multiple interacting technical layers, including:

  • database runtime
  • application processing
  • client connectivity
  • monitoring infrastructure
  • operating-system components
  • storage and network infrastructure
  • landscape-management components

 

Observable behavior does not automatically identify the originating technical component responsible for the situation.

For this reason, SAP troubleshooting documentation typically emphasizes monitoring interpretation, timeframe alignment, trace analysis, and correlation of technical observations before conclusions are validated.

Operational preparation therefore begins with collecting observable facts rather than immediately assigning root-cause responsibility.

Initial Self-Service Investigation
During the initial investigation phase, publicly available SAP resources may help clarify whether the observed behavior matches documented operational scenarios.

Examples may include:

  • SAP for Me knowledge search
  • SAP Help Portal documentation
  • SAP Notes
  • SAP Knowledge Base Articles
  • SAP HANA cockpit monitoring information
  • SAP HANA alerts
  • SAP HANA system views documentation
  • SAP Guided Answers
  • SAP Support Log Assistant, where applicable

These resources may help identify:

  • documented operational behavior
  • configuration prerequisites
  • monitoring interpretation guidance
  • operational limitations
  • affected technical areas
  • related troubleshooting documentation

This initial investigation phase may help narrow the technical scope before SAP Support involvement begins.

Suggested Initial Preparation Flow

When preparing an SAP HANA support incident, a structured preparation approach may help organize the observed situation and support clearer communication during investigation.

The following examples summarize commonly relevant preparation areas based on publicly available SAP troubleshooting concepts.

1. Define the Affected Scope
Examples may include:

  • single tenant or complete SAP HANA system
  • productive or non-productive environment
  • specific application or multiple applications
  • single host or multiple hosts
  • isolated user impact or broader landscape impact

Defining the affected scope may help align the investigation with the observed operational behavior.

2. Identify the Relevant Timeframe
Examples may include:

  • exact timestamp of the first observation
  • whether the behavior is continuous or intermittent
  • duration of the observed behavior
  • whether the issue started after:
    • maintenance activity
    • restart activity
    • takeover activity
    • upgrade or patching activity
    • infrastructure or network changes

Timeframe alignment is frequently important for monitoring interpretation, trace analysis, and correlation of observable system behavior.

3. Collect Observable Monitoring Information
Depending on the scenario, relevant observations may include:

  • active SAP HANA alerts
  • service status information
  • resource utilization observations
  • workload-related observations
  • replication status information
  • backup-related observations
  • connectivity-related observations

SAP HANA cockpit, SAP HANA monitoring tools, and SAP HANA system views may support the collection of this information.

The purpose of collecting monitoring information is to document observable system behavior during the affected timeframe.

4. Collect Relevant Diagnosis Information
Depending on the affected technical area, SAP Support may request diagnosis information relevant to the observed timeframe and system scope.

Examples may include diagnosis bundles, logs, traces, or full-system-info-dumps as described in SAP documentation and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

The relevance of diagnosis information depends on the technical scenario being investigated.

5. Document Already Performed Validation Steps
Examples may include:

  • restart attempts
  • connectivity validation
  • reproduction attempts
  • application-level observations
  • infrastructure-related observations
  • monitoring validation steps

Documenting already performed validation steps may help reduce ambiguity during investigation and support clearer communication between involved teams and SAP Support.

Monitoring and Evidence Collection
SAP HANA documentation describes monitoring and troubleshooting using alerts, monitoring tools, traces, diagnosis information, and related analysis resources.

Depending on the scenario, relevant observations may include:

  • SAP HANA alerts
  • service status information
  • workload-related observations
  • resource utilization indicators
  • replication status information
  • backup-related observations
  • connectivity-related observations

SAP HANA cockpit, monitoring interfaces, and SAP HANA diagnosis tools may support the collection and interpretation of this information.

Collected monitoring information may help provide additional technical context during investigation.

Diagnosis Files and Traces
SAP HANA troubleshooting documentation refers to diagnosis files, traces, logs, and related diagnostic tools as part of technical investigation.

Depending on the affected area, SAP Support may request diagnosis information relevant to the affected timeframe and technical scope.

SAP KBA 1732157 describes the collection of SAP HANA diagnosis information, including full-system-info-dumps.

 

The relevance of diagnosis information depends on the technical scenario being investigated.

Providing diagnosis information together with relevant operational context and timeframe details may help improve investigation alignment and interpretability during analysis.

Choosing the Appropriate SAP Component
The selected SAP support component should reflect the affected technical area as closely as possible.

Examples may include:

  • backup and recovery
  • system replication
  • performance
  • connectivity
  • SQL processing
  • security
  • installation and lifecycle management
  • cockpit and monitoring

Component selection should ideally reflect the observable technical area rather than an unvalidated root-cause assumption.

Working with SAP Support
SAP Support analysis is based on system-specific evidence collected from the affected environment.

Depending on the scenario, investigation activities may involve:

  • trace interpretation
  • monitoring correlation
  • workload analysis
  • topology evaluation
  • replication-state analysis
  • recovery validation
  • connectivity verification

Structured operational context may support clearer communication and investigation alignment during analysis.

Useful incident preparation may therefore include:

  • precise timestamps
  • reproducibility information
  • affected system scope
  • business impact
  • collected monitoring evidence
  • relevant diagnosis information
  • already performed validation steps


Conclusion
A structured operational context may help create a clearer shared understanding of the observed situation between involved teams and SAP Support.

SAP HANA investigations may require analysis across multiple technical layers, including database runtime, connectivity, infrastructure, monitoring, and application interaction.

Public SAP documentation and self-service resources may help identify documented operational behavior during the initial investigation phase.

When deeper analysis is required, organized operational context and relevant diagnosis information may support clearer communication, reduce unnecessary clarification cycles, and help investigation activities focus more efficiently on the affected technical area.

References

  • SAP for Me Knowledge Search
  • SAP HANA Troubleshooting and Performance Analysis Guide
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide for SAP HANA Platform
  • SAP HANA SQL and System Views Reference
  • SAP HANA Cockpit Documentation
  • SAP Guided Answers
  • SAP Support Log Assistant
  • SAP KBA 1732157 – Collecting diagnosis information for SAP HANA [VIDEO]


Disclaimer
This article summarizes publicly available SAP documentation and general troubleshooting concepts.

It does not replace SAP documentation, SAP Notes, SAP Knowledge Base Articles, or SAP Support procedures.

Only official SAP documentation and SAP Notes are authoritative.

entry.title: Shape What’s Next: SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026 Is Now Open
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that Cycle 2 of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative for 2026 is now live!
This cycle features 38 new projects, offering SAP customers and partners the opportunity to gain early insights into upcoming product developments and collaborate directly with SAP teams to shape the next generation of SAP solutions.

🗓Registration is open until June 19, 2026.

In a rapidly evolving business environment, early involvement can make a meaningful difference. Through the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative, you can work directly with SAP product managers and developers, sharing your feedback on concepts, prototypes, and plans while they are still in the early stages.

To help you explore all available opportunities, we’ve prepared a complete overview of the projects.
View the full list here, or browse the links below to discover the topics most relevant to you.

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • AI-Assisted Error Remediation for SAP Application Interface Framework
  • AI assisted guidance in the UI theme designer
  • BTP Specific Data Privacy & AI based Data Masking
  • Evolution of SAP BTP Runtimes
  • Fulfillment of business needs through Business Adaption Plans and Solution Implementation
  • Improving the Experience on SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry Runtime
  • Partner Perspectives in SAP BTP and AI development

Cloud ERP

  • Accelerate Integrations with AI powered SAP Integration Suite
  • Agentic AI in SAP S/4HANA Procurement (Private Cloud Edition)
  • Agentic AI Smart Billing Assistant
  • AI in SAP GRC for SAP HANA
  • AI-Powered Warranty Verification – Co-Innovate the future of service, repair & claims
  • Finance Intelligence on SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Intelligent Returns: Process Discovery & Automation Roadmap
  • Modernization Approaches in Purchase Order (SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition)
  • Parallel Unit of Measure

SAP Concur

  • Concur Invoice - AI-first, Reimagine Invoice Experience
  • Seamless configuration promotion for SAP Concur

SAP Globalization

  • AI for UI: Exploring the Usage of AI in Translating SAP's Product User Interface
  • Assessing Demand for AI Speech Services in SAP Translation Hub
  • PayTo Decoded: Let's Build It Together

Customer Success & Delivery

  • Improve SAP Cloud ALM Configuration & Security Analysis for SAP BTP and Cloud

Data & Analytics

  • SAP HANA Cloud Global Landscape Management
  • SAP HANA Cloud Intelligent Advisors
  • SAP HANA Database Migration to SAP HANA Cloud (XSC,XSA,CAP)

Lifecycle Management

  • Comprehensive Planning Assistant for Maintenance Planner
  • New version of SAP Add-On Assembly Kit

SAP Customer Engagement Initiative meets SAP Integration Experience Feedback

  • Hands-on: AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Mgmt - Provisioning & Setup
  • Hands-on Experience of AI-powered Dispute Mgmt Scenario with SAP Enterprise Service Management
  • Hands-on System Experience of the AI-Assisted Process Modeler in SAP Signavio

SAP Learning

  • Reimagining SAP Certification: Evolving a Skills-Based and Future-Ready Certification Experience

Others

  • Autonomous Supply Chain: Embodied AI in the Warehouse
  • Embodied AI Autonomous Asset Inspection
  • Embodied AI: Autonomous Visual Inspection for Quality and Safety
  • Iterate together on the User Experience for Joule Work
  • Providing feedback on the Discovery domain in SAP LeanIX
  • SAP Traceability Hub Repository
  • Support in Flow – Shaping Built-In Support Experience

Let’s shape the future together!

Innovation happens through collaboration. By participating in the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative projects, you help SAP create better, more relevant solutions for businesses like yours. This is your chance to influence what comes next.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2026!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Deploying SAP HANA 2.0 on AWS Using Terraform and Ansible
entry.content:
Introduction

This post is a trimmed-down version of the core automation work from my MSc final project — Improving Cloud Deployments with Automation Tools and React (available in full on figshare) — adapted here as a practical walkthrough for the SAP Community. The original project used AWS SSM for remote execution which, frankly, was a bit clunky; swapping it out for Ansible makes the whole thing considerably cleaner. I've left out the React UI (and Node) side of things — Terraform and Ansible are dyed-in-the-wool command line tools, and covering the UI layer in full would make this a very different (and much longer) post.

The goal was a fully automated SAP HANA 2.0 SPS08 deployment on AWS. Terraform provisions the infrastructure, Ansible handles the HANA installation.

Note: As this was originally a sandbox for a university project, everything is sized as small as possible for cost purposes. See below comments in the 'Minimum Viable Instance' section about sizing. 

Full repo: https://github.com/neilaspin/ansible_deployment

Prerequisites

You'll need the following installed locally:

  • Terraform >= 1.5
  • Ansible >= 2.14
  • AWS CLI v2
  • AWS credentials configured (aws configure or environment variables)
  • An AWS key pair created in your target region (us-east-2 in this guide)

On macOS, install Terraform and Ansible via Homebrew:

brew install terraform ansible awscli

Architecture

Local machine (terminal)
    ├── Terraform → provisions EC2 on AWS
    └── Ansible → installs HANA via SSH
                        │
                        ▼
                   AWS EC2 (SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG, r5.xlarge)
                        │
                        └── pulls HANA media from S3

Project Layout

hana-deployment/
├── terraform/
│   ├── providers.tf
│   ├── variables.tf
│   ├── main.tf
│   └── outputs.tf
└── ansible/
    ├── hana-install.yml
    └── templates/
        └── hana_install.cfg.j2

Terraform is run from the terraform/ directory, Ansible from the ansible/ directory.

Step 1 — Upload HANA Media to S3

Copy the HANA media up to S3. The EC2 instance pulls it down at install time using its IAM instance profile — no credentials needed on the instance.

aws s3 sync ~/Downloads/51058674/ s3://<your-s3-bucket>/SAP_HANA_DATABASE/ \
  --region us-east-2 --no-progress --exclude "*.DS_Store"

Once uploaded, the hdblcm installer will be reachable at:

s3://<your-s3-bucket>/SAP_HANA_DATABASE/DATA_UNITS/HDB_SERVER_LINUX_X86_64/hdblcm

Step 2 — Provision Infrastructure with Terraform

Why SUSE SLES for SAP PAYG?

HANA requires SUSE Linux Enterprise Server for SAP Applications — not standard SLES, not CentOS, not RHEL. On AWS, use the PAYG AMI. BYOS (bring your own subscription) requires a separate SUSE registration step that breaks unattended automation.

For us-east-2 (Ohio):

locals {
  sles_sap_ami = "ami-099afc29551c4b139"  # SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG, us-east-2
}

Minimum Viable Instance

SAP's minimum for HANA is 32GB RAM (you can go smaller, but it's a faff!). The smallest AWS instance that meets this is r5.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32GB). Not certified for production but acceptable for a sandbox.

Note:  The HANA data (50GB), log (25GB) and shared (50GB) filesystems each have their own dedicated EBS volumes, so the root volume only needs to accommodate the OS and the HANA installation media (~15GB). 50GB is sufficient. Remember this is a sandbox. Use proper SAP Sizing tools for productive use.

Terraform Files

providers.tf

terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
}

variables.tf

variable "aws_region" {
  default = "us-east-2"
}

variable "key_pair_name" {
  default = "<your-key-pair-name>"
}

variable "hana_media_bucket" {
  default = "<your-s3-bucket-name>"
}

main.tf

## SUSE SLES for SAP 15 SP5 PAYG (us-east-2)
locals {
  sles_sap_ami = "ami-099afc29551c4b139"
}

# IAM role so the instance can pull HANA media from S3
resource "aws_iam_role" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect    = "Allow"
      Principal = { Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com" }
      Action    = "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-read"
  role = aws_iam_role.hana_s3.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
      Resource = [
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}",
        "arn:aws:s3:::${var.hana_media_bucket}/*"
      ]
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "hana_s3" {
  name = "hana-s3-profile"
  role = aws_iam_role.hana_s3.name
}

resource "aws_security_group" "firewalld_test" {
  name        = "firewalld-test-sg"
  description = "SSH open - firewalld testing instance"

  ingress {
    description = "SSH from anywhere"
    from_port   = 22
    to_port     = 22
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "firewalld-test-sg"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "hana" {
  ami                    = local.sles_sap_ami
  instance_type          = "r5.xlarge"
  key_name               = var.key_pair_name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.firewalld_test.id]
  iam_instance_profile   = aws_iam_instance_profile.hana_s3.name

  root_block_device {
    volume_type = "gp3"
    volume_size = 50
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "hana-server"
  }
}

resource "aws_ebs_volume" "hana_data" {
  availability_zone = aws_instance.hana.availability_zone
  size              = 50
  type              = "gp3"
  tags              = { Name = "hana-data" }
}

resource "aws_ebs_volume" "hana_log" {
  availability_zone = aws_instance.hana.availability_zone
  size              = 25
  type              = "gp3"
  tags              = { Name = "hana-log" }
}

resource "aws_ebs_volume" "hana_shared" {
  availability_zone = aws_instance.hana.availability_zone
  size              = 50
  type              = "gp3"
  tags              = { Name = "hana-shared" }
}

resource "aws_volume_attachment" "hana_data" {
  device_name = "/dev/sdf"
  volume_id   = aws_ebs_volume.hana_data.id
  instance_id = aws_instance.hana.id
}

resource "aws_volume_attachment" "hana_log" {
  device_name = "/dev/sdg"
  volume_id   = aws_ebs_volume.hana_log.id
  instance_id = aws_instance.hana.id
}

resource "aws_volume_attachment" "hana_shared" {
  device_name = "/dev/sdh"
  volume_id   = aws_ebs_volume.hana_shared.id
  instance_id = aws_instance.hana.id
}

outputs.tf

output "public_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.hana.public_ip
}

output "ssh_command" {
  value = "ssh -i <your-key-pair-name>.pem ec2-user@${aws_instance.hana.public_ip}"
}

Run Terraform

terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply --auto-approve

Note the public IP from the output — you'll need it for the Ansible step:

terraform output public_ip

Step 3 — SSH Key Setup (macOS)

If you're on macOS, the PEM file needs to be in ~/.ssh/ with the quarantine flag removed, otherwise SSH will refuse to use it:

cp ~/Downloads/<your-key-pair-name>.pem ~/.ssh/
xattr -c ~/.ssh/<your-key-pair-name>.pem
chmod 400 ~/.ssh/<your-key-pair-name>.pem

Step 4 — Test SSH Connectivity

Before running the full playbook, verify Ansible can reach the instance:

ansible all \
  -i "ec2-user@<public-ip>," \
  --private-key ~/.ssh/<your-key-pair-name>.pem \
  -m ping \
  -e "ansible_ssh_common_args='-o StrictHostKeyChecking=no'"

Expected output:

<public-ip> | SUCCESS => {
    "changed": false,
    "ping": "pong"
}

Wait a minute or two after the instance starts before running this — cloud-init needs to finish first.

Step 5 — Install SAP HANA with Ansible

The hdblcm Response File

HANA's installer (hdblcm) supports unattended installation via a response file. We generate it with a Jinja2 template.

Gotcha: Use Ansible's template module, not copy. The copy module's YAML block scalar adds leading whitespace to each line that hdblcm's parser rejects.

hana_install.cfg.j2

[Server]
action=install
components=server
sid={{ hana_sid }}
number={{ hana_instance }}
password={{ hana_master_pw }}
sapadm_password={{ sapadm_password }}
system_user_password={{ hana_master_pw }}
lss_user_password={{ lss_password }}
lss_backup_password={{ lss_password }}
system_usage=custom
install_execution_mode=optimized
sapmnt={{ hana_install_dir }}
datapath={{ hana_data_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
logpath={{ hana_log_dir }}/{{ hana_sid }}
ignore=check_min_mem

Gotcha: lss_user_password and lss_backup_password are required from HANA 2.0 SPS06 onwards. LSS (Log Segment Store) was optional in earlier releases but is now mandatory. Omitting these fields causes hdblcm to fail with a cryptic parameter error. There is very little documentation about this online.

Run the Playbook

Run from the ansible/ directory, because that's where hana-install.yml and the templates/ folder live:

ansible-playbook \
  -i "ec2-user@<public-ip>," \
  --private-key ~/.ssh/<your-key-pair-name>.pem \
  -e "hana_s3_bucket=<your-s3-bucket> \
      hana_s3_prefix=SAP_HANA_DATABASE/DATA_UNITS/HDB_SERVER_LINUX_X86_64 \
      hana_sid=HDB \
      hana_instance=00 \
      hana_master_pw=<your-password> \
      sapadm_password=<your-password> \
      lss_password=<your-lss-password> \
      hana_media_dir=/hana/media" \
  -e "ansible_ssh_common_args='-o StrictHostKeyChecking=no -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null -o ServerAliveInterval=30 -o ServerAliveCountMax=20'" \
  hana-install.yml

The installation takes 20–40 minutes.

Verify

Once complete, SSH into the instance and check HANA is running:

ssh -i ~/.ssh/<your-key-pair-name>.pem ec2-user@<public-ip>
sudo su - hdbadm
HDB proc

A healthy HANA instance will show the following processes running under hdbadm: hdbdaemon, hdbnameserver, hdbcompileserver, hdbpreprocessor, hdbindexserver, hdbxsengine, hdbwebdispatcher, and sapstartsrv.

You should now be able to connect using hdbsql:

hdbsql -i 00 -u SYSTEM -p <your-password>

Step 6 — Tear Down

When you're done:

terraform destroy --auto-approve

This removes the EC2 instance, IAM role, instance profile, and security group. The S3 bucket and its contents are unmanaged by Terraform so they persist — you only pay S3 storage rates for the HANA media between runs.

Key Lessons

Problem Fix

BYOS AMI requires SUSE registrationUse PAYG AMI instead
SSH timeout kills hdblcmasync: 3600, poll: 30 in playbook
hdblcm config with leading spacesUse template module, not copy
lss_user_password missingRequired from SPS06+ — include it
hdblcm detects LSS media and failsMove lss folder aside before install
macOS SSH key permission deniedMove PEM to ~/.ssh/, xattr -c, chmod 400

Appendix — Full Ansible Playbook

The complete playbook from the original project is available on figshare.

hana-install.yml

---
- name: SAP HANA prerequisites and installation
  hosts: all
  become: true
  gather_facts: true

  vars:
    hana_sid: "HDB"
    hana_instance: "00"
    hana_master_pw: "<your-hana-master-password>"
    sapadm_password: "<your-sapadm-password>"
    lss_password: "<your-lss-password>"
    hana_s3_bucket: "<your-s3-bucket-name>"
    hana_s3_prefix: "SAP_HANA_DATABASE/DATA_UNITS/HDB_SERVER_LINUX_X86_64"
    hana_media_dir: "/hana/media"
    hana_install_dir: "/hana/shared"
    hana_data_dir: "/hana/data"
    hana_log_dir: "/hana/log"
    swap_size_mb: 20480

  tasks:

    - name: Confirm target is SUSE
      fail:
        msg: "This playbook requires SUSE Linux Enterprise Server. Got: {{ ansible_os_family }}"
      when: ansible_os_family != 'Suse'

    - name: Refresh zypper repositories
      zypper_repository:
        repo: '*'
        runrefresh: true
      ignore_errors: true

    - name: Install required OS packages
      zypper:
        name:
          - libaio1
          - libaio-devel
          - libltdl7
          - libatomic1
          - libnuma1
          - numactl
          - uuidd
          - rsync
          - lsof
          - unzip
          - xfsprogs
          - glibc-i18ndata
          - graphviz
          - expect
          - python3
        state: present

    - name: Enable and start uuidd
      systemd:
        name: uuidd
        state: started
        enabled: true

    - name: Check existing swap
      command: swapon --show=SIZE --noheadings --bytes
      register: swap_check
      changed_when: false
      failed_when: false

    - name: Create swapfile
      command: dd if=/dev/zero of=/swapfile bs=1M count={{ swap_size_mb }}
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Set swapfile permissions
      file:
        path: /swapfile
        mode: "0600"
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Format swapfile
      command: mkswap /swapfile
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Activate swapfile
      command: swapon /swapfile
      when: swap_check.stdout == ""

    - name: Persist swapfile in fstab
      lineinfile:
        path: /etc/fstab
        line: "/swapfile swap swap defaults 0 0"
        state: present

    - name: Set kernel parameters for HANA
      sysctl:
        name: "{{ item.key }}"
        value: "{{ item.value }}"
        sysctl_set: true
        state: present
        reload: true
      loop:
        - { key: "vm.max_map_count",            value: "2147483647" }
        - { key: "kernel.sem",                   value: "1250 256000 100 8192" }
        - { key: "net.core.somaxconn",           value: "4096" }
        - { key: "net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog", value: "8192" }
        - { key: "kernel.numa_balancing",        value: "0" }

    - name: Set ulimits for SAP
      copy:
        dest: /etc/security/limits.d/99-sap.conf
        mode: "0644"
        content: |
          @sapsys  soft  nofile  1048576
          @sapsys  hard  nofile  1048576
          @sapsys  soft  nproc   unlimited
          @sapsys  hard  nproc   unlimited
          @sdba    soft  nofile  1048576
          @sdba    hard  nofile  1048576

    - name: Disable THP at runtime
      shell: |
        echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
        echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag
      changed_when: true

    - name: Create systemd unit to disable THP on boot
      copy:
        dest: /etc/systemd/system/disable-thp.service
        mode: "0644"
        content: |
          [Unit]
          Description=Disable Transparent Huge Pages
          After=network.target
          [Service]
          Type=oneshot
